evidence: buildExploreResponseBody and buildBaseExploreResponse centralize shared response assembly for both single-seed and multi-seed paths, so the handler seam has no duplication
All model-to-JSON translation (formatDiscoveredNoun, formatCoreCollocate, formatAdjectiveProfile, formatEdge) is concentrated inside ExplorationHandlers, not leaked into model or query packages
ExploreOptions record bundles tuning parameters cleanly, removing a 5-argument list from the exploreByPattern call site
CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates is a shared ranking seam reused verbatim by findCollocations, executeDependencyPattern, and executeSurfacePattern, keeping logDice scoring in one place

exploreMultiSeed in SemanticFieldExplorer maps multi-seed results into ExplorationResult with seed set to String.join(',', seeds); ExplorationResult.getSeed() then returns 'theory,model,hypothesis', a string that does not represent a single seed — the handler must compensate by adding separate 'seeds' and 'seed_count' keys outside the standard buildExploreResponseBody path

In BlackLabQueryExecutor.findCollocations, when BlackLabSnippetParser.extractLemmaFromMatch returns null, the fallback uses ad-hoc lastIndexOf(' ') string splitting (trimmed.substring(lastSpace + 1)) rather than any shared utility; executeDependencyPattern has no equivalent fallback, so extraction strategy is inconsistent across methods at the executor/parser boundary

ExplorationResult.getEdges() and ComparisonResult.getEdges() build visualization edges directly in model classes, embedding edge-type strings ('seed_adj', 'discovered_adj', 'modifier') that are presentation concerns; the handler then calls result.getEdges() and re-maps through formatEdge(), meaning edge construction logic is split between model and formatter

SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples builds a hardcoded BCQL pattern '[lemma="%s" & xpos="JJ.*"] []{0,3} [lemma="%s" & xpos="NN.*"]' inline, bypassing RelationConfig.getFullPattern() entirely; every other exploration method delegates to RelationConfig, so this method silently breaks the config→explorer contract

strengths: Translation at the handler boundary is consolidated: all format* helpers are package-private statics in ExplorationHandlers, so domain objects never carry JSON-map building logic
buildExploreResponseBody extracts the shared response structure shared by single-seed and multi-seed handlers, avoiding divergent copy-paste at the HTTP seam
ExploreOptions record eliminates a long parameter list at the SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern seam, making the handoff explicit and minimal
CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates is a clean shared scoring seam reused by all three executor query variants without duplication
HttpApiUtils.requireParam provides a consistent null-sentinel gateway pattern for parameter extraction, with a predictable single-check protocol across all handlers
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are concentrated at two seams — the multi-seed data mapping into a single-seed-shaped result type, and a pair of bypasses (ad-hoc extraction fallback, hardcoded BCQL in fetchExamples) that create invisible inconsistencies at the executor/config boundaries — rather than being a systemic breakdown across the whole module.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase sits above the 60-point drag threshold: boundary translation is generally disciplined, helpers are well-factored, and the three-layer handoff (API → exploration → query) is legible. However, the ExplorationResult reuse for multi-seed data forces the handler to patch around the model's semantic mismatch, and two config/extraction bypasses create invisible seam inconsistencies that a developer will stumble on. These are not isolated micro-issues — each affects how collaborators communicate — but they are bounded to specific seams rather than pervasive, which keeps the score from falling further. Solid-but-uneven lands around 67.

issues.summary: exploreMultiSeed maps multi-seed data into single-seed ExplorationResult, forcing the handler to compensate with extra keys outside the shared response path
evidence: exploreMultiSeed returns new ExplorationResult(String.join(',', seeds), ...) — getSeed() returns 'theory,model,hypothesis', not a single seed

handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti calls buildExploreResponseBody(response, result) but then adds response.put('seeds', ...) and response.put('seed_count', ...) outside of it, because the shared helper only writes the single-seed key 'seed' on the single-seed path
The seeds become discoveredNouns inside ExplorationResult, even though they are known inputs, not discovered outputs — the type's meaning is semantically stretched
issues.suggestion: Introduce a dedicated MultiSeedExplorationResult (or add a seeds: List<String> field to ExplorationResult alongside seed) so the type correctly carries multi-seed data, and buildExploreResponseBody can handle both cases uniformly without out-of-band handler patching.

issues.summary: findCollocations uses an ad-hoc lastIndexOf(' ') fallback for lemma extraction not shared with executeDependencyPattern, scattering identity-parsing logic across two call sites
evidence: In findCollocations: if extractLemmaFromMatch returns null, the code does trimmed.lastIndexOf(' ') and takes the suffix — raw string splitting with no encapsulation
executeDependencyPattern calls extractCollocateLemma(identity) with no fallback — the two methods handle a null result differently with no shared strategy
BlackLabSnippetParser already has extractCollocateLemma and extractLemmaAt for positional extraction, so the fallback is bypassing the canonical extraction seam
issues.suggestion: Move the fallback strategy into BlackLabSnippetParser (e.g., extractLemmaFromMatchWithFallback), so all executor methods share one extraction seam and the fallback logic is visible and testable in one place.

issues.summary: ExplorationResult.getEdges() and ComparisonResult.getEdges() embed visualization edge construction — including presentation-level edge type strings — inside model classes
evidence: ExplorationResult.getEdges() creates Edge objects with type 'seed_adj' and 'discovered_adj' — these string labels are visualization/presentation conventions, not domain invariants
ComparisonResult.getEdges() similarly creates Edge('modifier') labels inside the model
The handler then iterates result.getEdges() and re-maps each Edge through formatEdge(), so the model builds edges and the handler formats them, splitting the boundary logic across two layers
issues.suggestion: Remove getEdges() from both model classes and move edge construction into ExplorationHandlers (or a dedicated EdgeBuilder), so model classes carry only data and the handler owns the full model-to-response translation in one place.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples hardcodes a BCQL pattern instead of delegating to RelationConfig, silently breaking the grammar-config contract
evidence: fetchExamples builds: "[lemma=\"%s\" & xpos=\"JJ.*\"] []{0,3} [lemma=\"%s\" & xpos=\"NN.*\"]" — hardcoded xpos tags and window size with no config injection
Every other exploration method uses relationConfig.getFullPattern(seed) or relationConfig.collocateReversePattern() to look up patterns from grammar config
The API handler (handleSemanticFieldExamples) does not take a relation parameter, so there is no way to vary the pattern even if a caller wanted to
issues.suggestion: Accept a RelationConfig parameter in fetchExamples (or use a default config from grammarConfig) and derive the example query from it, consistent with all other exploration methods. Alternatively, expose the hardcoded pattern as a named constant with a comment explaining why it is relation-agnostic.

root_causes: ExplorationResult was designed for single-seed use and reused for multi-seed without evolving the type, creating an impedance mismatch at the model boundary
BlackLabSnippetParser's extraction utilities were added incrementally rather than as a single canonical API, leaving fallback logic to grow in call sites
SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples was likely added as a quick utility without integrating it into the grammar-config-driven pattern used by the rest of the class
